elements.sent-2411-001: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have made significant advancements, demonstrating powerful capabilities in processing and understanding multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-002: Fine-tuning MLLMs with Federated Learning (FL) allows for expanding the training data scope by including private data sources, thereby enhancing their practical applicability in privacy-sensitive domains.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, current research remains in the early stage, particularly in addressing the multimodal heterogeneities in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we introduce a benchmark for evaluating various downstream tasks in the federated fine-tuning of MLLMs within multimodal heterogeneous scenarios, laying the groundwork for the research in the field.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our benchmark encompasses two datasets, five comparison baselines, and four multimodal scenarios, incorporating over ten types of modal heterogeneities.
elements.sent-2411-006: To address the challenges posed by modal heterogeneity, we develop a general FedMLLM framework that integrates four representative FL methods alongside two modality-agnostic strategies.
elements.sent-2411-007: Extensive experimental results show that our proposed FL paradigm improves the performance of MLLMs by broadening the range of training data and mitigating multimodal heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-009: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), which regard Large Language Models (LLMs) as core components for performing various multimodal tasks, have rapidly emerged as a research hotspot~\cite{yin2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Their exceptional performance and potential as a pathway to artificial general intelligence contribute to this trend.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the high computational costs and the requirement for large amounts of training data are significant challenges that hinder the further development and application of MLLMs in the real world~\cite{yao2024minicpm, liu2023visual .}
elements.sent-2411-012: For the former, various lightweight MLLMs~\cite{yao2024minicpm, li2024mini and Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)~\cite{hu2021lora, lester2021power methods, such as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)~\cite{hu2021lora , have been investigated.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: For the latter, a practical solution is to extend the data scope from public datasets to unused private data via Federated Learning (FL)~\cite{mcmahan2017communication, kairouz2021advances .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{Idea-v1.pdf} \caption{Comparison between our MLLMs$+$FL and previous LLMs$+$FL. MLLMs$+$FL supports a broader range of modalities compared to LLMs$+$FL, enabling more comprehensive applications and improved performance in tasks requiring multimodal modeling. Nevertheless, the challenges from multimodal heterogeneities need to be addressed via modality-agnostic strategies.} \label{fig:idea}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{Overview.pdf} \caption{Overview of FedMLLM and its performance show. FedMLLM has fully deployed over ten multimodal heterogeneities, four representative FL algorithms, two modality-agnostic strategies, and supports two downstream datasets, five comparison baselines, and four multimodal scenarios. The results of the multimodal scenarios come from federated fine-tuning of MLLM on the Hateful-Memes (H) and CrisisMMD (C) datasets, with two modality-agnostic strategies improving MLLM training in more challenging, non-aligned modal scenarios. Better view in $\times$3 PDF version.} \label{fig:fedmllm}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, there is limited research on fine-tuning MLLMs under the FL framework~\cite{zhang2024mllm , with most efforts focused on integrating LLMs with FL~\cite{ye2024openfedllm, kuang2024federatedscope, zhang2024towards .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Compared to LLMs$+$FL, MLLMs$+$FL supports more modal inputs, making it more applicable to real-world scenarios~\cite{yin2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, in natural disasters or emergency situations (e.g., earthquakes, floods), it is essential to integrate multi-modal data such as images or videos (on-site photos), text (social media reports), and audio (distress signals).
elements.sent-2411-017: MLLMs+FL enable distributed collaboration across organizations while safeguarding data privacy, offering a more comprehensive understanding of disasters through multi-modal modeling.
elements.sent-2411-018: In contrast, LLMs+FL are limited to text, missing key information from images and audio.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, the multimodal data, as the input of FL framework of multi-client collaborative training, also brings about more complex modal heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-020: As shown in~\cref{fig:idea , multimodal scenarios can be categorized as the aligned modality~\cite{feng2023fedmultimodal , and the non-aligned modality (i.e., missing modality~\cite{ma2021smil, che2024leveraging , cross modality~\cite{yang2024cross , and hybrid modality~\cite{yumultimodal, chen2022fedmsplit ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Only the heterogeneity of the aligned modality is consistent with that of a single modality, while the non-aligned multimodal situations exhibit additional modal heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-022: To eliminate the bias caused by modal heterogeneity, we design two simple yet effective modal-agnostic strategies based on existing FL methods, as illustrated in~\cref{fig:fedmllm .}
elements.sent-2411-023: First, the prompt strategy instructs LLMs to perform tasks without considering modality, e.g., ``..., without considering the modality.", which considers the effect of instructions on LLMs at the data level~\cite{touvron2023llama .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Second, the regularization strategy constrains the update direction.
elements.sent-2411-025: As the client moves from aligned to missing modalities, eventually to extreme single-modality cases, the risk of overfitting to a specific modality gradually increases.
elements.sent-2411-026: To address this, the adaptive regularization strategy adjusts consistency with global weights based on the severity of missing modalities, with stronger consistency for more missing modalities.
elements.sent-2411-027: Meanwhile, only the middle layers are regularized, preserving the ability of the low and high layers to extract modal features and provide task-specific representations.
elements.sent-2411-028: Combined with the above modal-agnostic strategies, we comprehensively explore the pipeline of federated fine-tuning of MLLM on decentralized multimodal downstream data.
elements.sent-2411-029: Unlike~\cite{zhang2024mllm , which applies MLLM solely to assist FL in alleviating traditional data heterogeneity and long-tail distribution problems, we focus more on addressing the issue of diverse modal heterogeneity in multimodal scenarios that are closely related to MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-030: As shown in~\cref{fig:fedmllm , the proposed pipeline supports four multimodal scenarios: i.~Aligned Modal Scenario refers to all samples of each client existing in an aligned multimodal form.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In total, we construct over ten cases of modal heterogeneity across two datasets, based on varying heterogeneity degrees and the proportions of non-aligned multi-modalities.
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, the application of FL in MLLMs significantly improves performance compared to local training, as shown in the four modal results of~\cref{fig:fedmllm .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Additionally, the two modality-agnostic strategies proposed for the modal heterogeneity problem also contribute positively.
elements.sent-2411-034: Overall, our contributions can be summarized in three folds.
elements.sent-2411-035: The rapid development of Large Language Models (LLMs) has significantly driven the research surge in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)~\cite{yin2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Notably, the emergence of commercial models such as GPT-4~\cite{openai2022chatgpt and Gemini~\cite{team2023gemini has attracted widespread attention.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Simultaneously, increasingly powerful open-source models~\cite{li2023blip, chiang2023vicuna, zhu2023minigpt have also emerged.}
elements.sent-2411-038: BLIP-2~\cite{li2023blip is well-known for its Q-former module, with promising VQA results.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Building on BLIP-2 and Vicuna~\cite{chiang2023vicuna , MiniGPT-4~\cite{zhu2023minigpt , derived from GPT-4~\cite{openai2022chatgpt , is proposed for image-text pairs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Unlike previous methods, LLaVA~\cite{liu2023visual is trained on multimodal instruction data generated by GPT-4 to enhance its zero-shot capability.}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, the large number of parameters in MLLMs leads to high computational costs for training and deployment, limiting their broader application.
elements.sent-2411-042: Recently, efficient MLLMs have also gained significant attention within the academia~\cite{jin2024efficient , with representative models including Phi~\cite{javaheripi2023phi , Gemma~\cite{banks2024gemma , MobileLLM~\cite{liu2024mobilellm , and MiniCPM-V~\cite{yao2024minicpm .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Compared to previous approaches, MiniCPM-V~\cite{yao2024minicpm optimizes architecture, training, and inference to achieve superior performance with fewer parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Consequently, we utilize MiniCPM-V as the MLLM for both the client and server in the federated learning process.
elements.sent-2411-045: As a classic framework for privacy protection, Federated Learning (FL) typically allows multiple parties to co-train while keeping the data local~\cite{huang2024federated .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The original FedAvg~\cite{mcmahan2017communication method aggregates model weights from different clients solely based on sample proportions, which makes it challenging to effectively address complex data heterogeneity.}
elements.sent-2411-047: As a result, various variants have emerged: some constrain the update direction of the local model on the client side~\cite{li2020federated, karimireddy2020scaffold , while others optimize the aggregation method on the server side~\cite{reddiadaptive, hsu2019measuring, rehman2023dawa .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Our benchmark includes four representative FL methods for server-side aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-049: FedYogi, FedAdam, and FedAdagrad~\cite{reddiadaptive apply the centralized Yogi~\cite{zaheer2018adaptive , Adam~\cite{kingma2014adam , and Adagrad~\cite{duchi2011adaptive optimizers to the server-side aggregation in the FL framework, while FedAvgm~\cite{hsu2019measuring uses momentum updates on the server, similar to momentum applied on top of SGD.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Because the original FedAvg~\cite{mcmahan2017communication and FedProx~\cite{li2020federated with a global regularization term face training instability during federated fine-tuning of MLLM, the conventional average aggregation method does not converge effectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: As a result, these two methods are not included separately in the comparison baselines.
elements.sent-2411-052: Unlike the more established single-modal FL, Multimodal Federated Learning (MFL) has significant potential for exploration~\cite{feng2023fedmultimodal .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Existing MFL methods can also be categorized based on the different modal scenarios in our benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-054: In aligned modal scenarios, MFL techniques resemble those of single-modal FL, focusing on client-specific and shared knowledge~\cite{chen2024feddat .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Some methods also draw from multimodal learning, especially in feature fusion~\cite{feng2023fedmultimodal .}
elements.sent-2411-056: In missing modal scenarios, some MFL methods rely on pre-trained generative models to fill the gaps in modality~\cite{che2024leveraging .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In cross modal scenarios, some MFL methods employ a two-branch structure to separately constrain the learning of specific and shared modalities~\cite{yang2024cross,yang2022cross,qi2024adaptive , while hybrid modal MFL methods utilize various graph techniques to capture complex correlations among clients~\cite{chen2022fedmsplit,chen2024disentanglement .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Typically, MFL extracts features from raw inputs using pre-trained models before training, but its performance is ultimately limited by the effectiveness of these models in feature extraction~\cite{feng2023fedmultimodal,yang2024cross .}
elements.sent-2411-059: With the rise of MLLMs, their application in MFL has become a trend.
elements.sent-2411-060: However, only one study so far has attempted to combine MLLMs with FL on the server side to address issues of data heterogeneity and long-tail distribution~\cite{zhang2024mllm .}
elements.sent-2411-061: In this paper, we present the first benchmark for federated fine-tuning of MLLMs, targeting diverse multimodal heterogeneities.

elements.sent-2411-063: As shown in~\cref{fig:fedmllm , the simulation is divided into four scenarios: Aligned Modal Scenario, Missing Modal Scenario, Cross Modal Scenario, and Hybrid Modal Scenario.. Aligned Modal Scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The dataset is sampled using Dirichlet distribution, similar to the traditional partitioning method~\cite{feng2023fedmultimodal , with coefficients $\alpha=\{5.0, 1.0, 0.5\}$ adjusted to control the distribution of samples among different clients.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Specifically, for each label, Dirichlet distribution is used to allocate the number of samples to each client; the smaller the coefficient, the greater the difference in sample sizes between clients, resulting in increased data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-066: Here, each client’s sample contains all modalities.. Missing Modal Scenario.
elements.sent-2411-067: The dataset first assigns samples to different clients according to the partitioning strategy of aligned modal scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-068: Currently, the samples in each client are full-modal.
elements.sent-2411-069: By using a uniform distribution for each modality and setting a missing rate of $\beta(\%)=\{30, 40, 50\}$ to determine whether to remove that modality, the situation where the samples in each client have different missing modalities is achieved.. Cross Modal Scenario.
elements.sent-2411-070: The dataset is first allocated according to a partitioning strategy similar to the aligned modal scenario and then divided based on the ratio (\{$\text{I- 3:\text{T- 7$, $\text{I- 5:\text{T- 5$, $\text{I- 7:\text{T- 3$\}) of image-only to text-only clients.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: For example, in the $\text{I- 3:\text{T- 7$ ratio, 3 out of 10 clients are randomly assigned the image modality, while the other 7 clients receive the text modality.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The other ratios follow the same pattern.
elements.sent-2411-073: This approach allows each client to have a single modality while ensuring that all clients collectively have multiple modalities.. Hybrid Modal Scenario.
elements.sent-2411-074: After applying a partitioning method consistent with the aligned modality scenario, the dataset follows a Bernoulli distribution for each client's modality retention probability $p(\%)=\{80, 70, 60\}$, which is the same for each modality.
elements.sent-2411-075: Once the partitioning is complete, a single client may consist of either a single modality or a combination of multiple modalities, with internal samples maintaining consistency in modality.
elements.sent-2411-076: To avoid an invalid dataset missing both modalities on the client side, in such cases, either the image or text modality is randomly retained with a 50\% probability.

elements.sent-2411-080: As shown in the architecture part of~\cref{fig:fedmllm , the proposed FedMLLM is divided into two parts: the cloud (server-side) and the terminal (client-side).}
elements.sent-2411-081: Both parts use the same MLLM~\cite{yao2024minicpm and share a homogeneous model architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-082: In FedMLLM, we address an optimization problem similar to that of traditional FL~\cite{mcmahan2017communication , as follows, \begin{equation}\min_{w\in\mathbb{R}^d}f(w)=\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^KF_k(w)\end{equation} where $F_k(w)$ represents the loss function of client $k$ among a total of $K$ clients, specifically given by $F_k(w)=\mathbb{E _{x\sim \mathcal{D _k [f_k(w,x)]$, with $\mathcal{D _k$ denoting the data distribution for the $k^{\text{th }$ client.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Here, the model parameters are given by $w=w_0+\Delta w=w_0+BA$, where $w_0$ refers to the frozen pretrained weights, and $BA$ is the trained LoRA component~\cite{hu2021lora of the LLM.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The FL process of FedMLLM starts on the server side, which sends either the initialized LoRA weights (in the first round) or the aggregated weights (in subsequent rounds) to the selected clients to initialize their LoRA weights.
elements.sent-2411-085: Then, client $k$ fine-tunes the initialized LoRA weights on the local dataset $\mathcal{P _k=\{(\mathcal{I ^i,\mathcal{M ^i,\mathcal{R ^i)\}_i^{n_k}$, where $\mathcal{I ^i$, $\mathcal{M ^i$, $\mathcal{R ^i$ denote the instruction token sequences, multimodal input token sequences, and ground truth response token sequences, respectively, with $n_k=|\mathcal{P _k|$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The loss function~\cite{yin2023survey maximizes the probability of the current input sequence generating the next correct token, as follows, \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{mllm}=-\log\prod_{l=1}^{L^i}p(\mathcal{R}_l^i|\mathcal{M}^i,\mathcal{I}^i,\mathcal{R}^i_{<l};w^{(t,r)}_k)\end{equation} where $L^i$ is the length of $\mathcal{R ^i$, and $w^{(t,r) _k$ represents the model parameters of client $k$ at epoch $r$ of round $t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Once local training is complete, the updated LoRA weights from the selected clients are uploaded to the server for aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-088: At this stage, one training round has been completed, and the FL training process needs to be repeated for several more rounds until the model converges.
elements.sent-2411-089: In decentralized multimodal data scenarios, different modality conditions among clients further complicate the data heterogeneity problem.
elements.sent-2411-090: Lacking modalities may introduce additional modality bias into the model's learning process, affecting the convergence of the global model.
elements.sent-2411-091: A common solution is to address this issue at the data source by using a large-scale pre-trained model to supplement the modality~\cite{che2024leveraging .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Nevertheless, it often incurs additional costs.
elements.sent-2411-093: To avoid these costs, we aim to mitigate the impact of modality changes on task outcomes by explicitly prompting model training.
elements.sent-2411-094: Inspired by this, we augment the original prompt by introducing a modality-agnostic prompt strategy to enhance the model's robustness and generalization ability, as shown in~\cref{fig:org-aug .. Regularization Strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-095: The regularization term originally introduced by FedProx~\cite{li2020federated constrains the direction of local updates toward the global model, serving as a general method for mitigating heterogeneity.}
elements.sent-2411-096: However, the average aggregation strategies used in FedProx and FedAvg~\cite{mcmahan2017communication can exacerbate instability during federated fine-tuning of MLLM, leading to a gradual increase in loss and its variance (see in Supplementary Material).}
elements.sent-2411-097: In contrast, FedAvgM employs server momentum~\cite{hsu2019measuring , while FedAdam, FedYogi, and FedAdagrad use adaptive optimizers~\cite{reddiadaptive , allowing them to handle instability more effectively.}}

elements.sent-2411-098: To reduce the negative effect of modal heterogeneity, a straightforward approach is to integrate the FedProx regularization term into the stable FL baselines.
elements.sent-2411-099: This traditional regularization strategy with a fixed coefficient is not suitable for various non-aligned modal scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-100: Specifically, as the issue of missing modalities becomes more severe in such scenarios, it is more reasonable to strengthen the regularization to further prevent overfitting to a particular modality.
elements.sent-2411-101: At the same time, to preserve both the low-level modality-specific and high-level task-specific information, we only regularize the intermediate modality-agnostic layers.
elements.sent-2411-102: Therefore, an adaptive regularization strategy is presented, as follows, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{mllm} + \gamma\cdot\mathcal{A}\cdot\|\Delta\tilde{w}-\Delta w_k\|^2 \mathcal{A} = [\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{S},1,\dots,1, \underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{S}]^\top \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\gamma$ is an adaptive coefficient, $\mathcal{A}$ is the layer-wise mask, $\top$ is the transpose operation, $S$ is a layer-count hyperparameter, and $\Delta\tilde{w}$ represents the server-side aggregated LoRA weight.
elements.sent-2411-103: In cross modal scenario, suppose $\gamma$ is set to $\gamma_{\text{max }$.}
elements.sent-2411-104: When the missing rate is $\beta\%$ in missing modal scenario, $\gamma$ is linearly adjusted to $\gamma_{\text{max \times \beta\%$ to adaptively adjust the strength of agreement with the global model.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: The hybrid modal scenario combines aligned, missing, and cross modal scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-106: Thus, the $\gamma$ setting follows these scenarios, with no regularization in the aligned case.
elements.sent-2411-107: In practice, $\beta\%$ is calculated from the client's private dataset.

elements.sent-2411-110: Moreover, social media information from different regions and privacy levels has driven research on FL in this field.
elements.sent-2411-111: Therefore, in evaluating the FedMLLM framework, we utilize two multimodal social media datasets, Hateful-Memes~\cite{kiela2020hateful and CrisisMMD~\cite{alam2018crisismmd , containing image-text pairs.}}

elements.sent-2411-174: In this paper, we introduce a benchmark to evaluate the performance of federated fine-tuning of MLLMs across various multimodal heterogeneous scenarios, laying the groundwork for future research in the field.
elements.sent-2411-175: Our benchmark includes two lightweight MLLMs, two downstream tasks, three evaluation metrics, and five datasets across three domains, along with six comparison baselines, covering over ten types of modality heterogeneities across four multimodal scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-176: To address the challenges posed by multimodal heterogeneity, we develop a general FedMLLM framework that integrates classic FL methods alongside two modality-agnostic strategies.
elements.sent-2411-177: Code is available in supplementary materials.
elements.sent-2411-178: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), which use Large Language Models (LLMs) as core components for various multimodal tasks, have rapidly emerged as a research hotspot~\cite{yin2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-179: The exceptional performance of MLLMs and their potential as a pathway to artificial general intelligence have driven this trend.
elements.sent-2411-180: However, the decreasing amount of available public data~\cite{villalobos2024position is an urgent issue that hinders the further development and application of MLLMs in the real world~\cite{yao2024minicpm, liu2023visual .}}
elements.sent-2411-181: To expand the available data scope, a practical solution is to fine-tune existing MLLMs, trained on public data, with unused private data via Federated Learning (FL)~\cite{mcmahan2017communication, kairouz2021advances .}
